The syntax of the radius-server retries command is: radius-server retries <0-5>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the radius-server timeout command do?

The radius-server timeout command specifies the number of seconds to wait for a response from the RADIUS server before trying the next RADIUS server. If a value is not specified, a default value of 5 seconds is used. You can override this value with a fine-grained per server timeout configured for individual servers. The no form of this command resets the RADIUS global authentication timeout to the default of 5 seconds. Syntax: radius-server timeout [<1-60>].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the radius-server timeout command?
The syntax of the radius-server timeout command is: radius-server timeout [<1-60>]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the radius-server tls timeout (RadSec) command do?

The radius-server tls timeout (RadSec) command specifies the number of seconds to wait for a response from the RadSec server before trying the next RADIUS or RadSec server. If a value is not specified, a default value of 5 seconds is used. You can override this value with a fine-grained per server timeout configured for individual servers. The no form of this command resets the RadSec global authentication timeout to the default of 5 seconds. Syntax: radius-server tls timeout [<1-60>].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the radius-server tls timeout (RadSec) command?
The syntax of the radius-server tls timeout (RadSec) command is: radius-server tls timeout [<1-60>]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the server {<FQDN> | <IPv4> | <IPv6>} [tls] [port <PORT-NUMBER>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The server {<FQDN> | <IPv4> | <IPv6>} [tls] [port <PORT-NUMBER>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command adds a TACACS+/RADIUS server to a server-group. Only the configured TACACS+/RADIUS servers are allowed to be added within the server group. If the same server name exists with multiple ports or multiple VRFs, specify the server name, port, and VRF when adding the server to the server-group. The no form of this command removes a TACACS+/RADIUS server from a server-group. Syntax: server {<FQDN> | <IPv4> | <IPv6>} [tls] [port <PORT-NUMBER>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the server {<FQDN> | <IPv4> | <IPv6>} [tls] [port <PORT-NUMBER>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command?
The syntax of the server {<FQDN> | <IPv4> | <IPv6>} [tls] [port <PORT-NUMBER>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command is: server {<FQDN> | <IPv4> | <IPv6>} [tls] [port <PORT-NUMBER>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>]

The show aaa server-groups command shows TACACS+ and RADIUS AAA server group information for all server types or for the specified server type. Syntax: show aaa server-groups [tacacs | radius] [vsx-peer].

The syntax of the show aaa server-groups command is: show aaa server-groups [tacacs | radius] [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show accounting log command do?

The show accounting log command entered without optional parameters, this command shows all accounting log records for the current boot. Sensitive information is masked from the log, by being represented as asterisks. NOTE: This show accounting log command replaces the show audit-log command that is supported only in 10.00 releases. Syntax: show accounting log [last <QTY-TO-SHOW> | all].

The syntax of the show accounting log command is: show accounting log [last <QTY-TO-SHOW> | all]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show radius-server command do?
The show radius-server command shows configured RADIUS servers information. Syntax: show radius-server [detail] [vsx-peer].

The syntax of the show radius-server command is: show radius-server [detail] [vsx-peer]

The show radius-server statistics command shows authentication statistics for all configured RADIUS servers. Syntax: show radius-server statistics authentication [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show radius-server statistics command?
The syntax of the show radius-server statistics command is: show radius-server statistics authentication [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show tacacs-server statistics command do?
The show tacacs-server statistics command shows authentication statistics for all configured TACACS+ servers. Syntax: show tacacs-server statistics [vsx-peer].

The syntax of the show tacacs-server statistics command is: show tacacs-server statistics [vsx-peer]

The show tech aaa command shows the AAA configuration settings. Syntax: show tech aaa.

The syntax of the show tech aaa command is: show tech aaa.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the tacacs-server auth-type command do?

The tacacs-server auth-type command enables the CHAP or PAP authentication protocol, which is used for communication with the TACACS+ servers, at the global level. You can override this command with a fine-grained per server auth-type configuration. The no form of this command resets the global authentication mechanism for TACACS+ to PAP, which is the default authentication mechanism for TACACS+. Syntax: tacacs-server auth-type {pap | chap}.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the tacacs-server auth-type command?
The syntax of the tacacs-server auth-type command is: tacacs-server auth-type {pap | chap}.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the tacacs-server timeout command do?

The tacacs-server timeout command specifies the number of seconds to wait for a response from the TACACS+ server before trying the next TACACS+ server. If a value is not specified, a default value of 5 seconds is used. You can override this value with a fine-grained per server timeout configured for individual servers. The no form of this command resets the TACACS+ global authentication timeout to the default of 5 seconds. Syntax: tacacs-server timeout [<1-60>].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the tacacs-server timeout command?
The syntax of the tacacs-server timeout command is: tacacs-server timeout [<1-60>]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what security policy information is documented for PKI?

PKI: The public key infrastructure (PKI) feature enables administrators to manage digital certificates on the switch. The switch uses certificates to validate SSH clients when acting as an SSH server, and when communicating with syslog servers when TLS encryption is used. PKI concepts Digital certificate A digital certificate is an electronic form of identification that stores important information about an entity (such as a computer, program, or website). Certificates help secure digital transactions by enabling the end parties to validate each other's identity. Digital certificates are issued by a certificate authority (CA) and are composed of an encoded string of characters (usually stored in a file). For example: -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- MIIDsDCCApgCCQDJotuPPj9GCDANBgkqhkiG9w0BAQsAADCBqzELMAkGA1UEBh VVMx EzARBgNVBAgMCk Nhb Glmb3JuaWExEDAOBgNVBAcBM1JvY2tsaW4xDDAKBg BAoMA0hQTjEVMBMGA1UECwwMSFBOUm9zZXZpb GxlMSokwAYDVQQDDCFocG5zdz...
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the crypto pki application command do?

The crypto pki application command associates a leaf certificate with a feature (application) on the switch. By default, all features are associated with the default, self-signed certificate local-cert. This certificate is created by the switch the first time it starts. The no form of this command associates the specified feature with the default certificate. Syntax: crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the crypto pki application command?
The syntax of the crypto pki application command is: crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the crypto pki ta-profile command do?

The crypto pki ta-profile command creates a trust anchor (TA) profile and changes to the config-ta-<TA-NAME> context for the profile. Each TA profile stores the certificate for a trusted CA. Up to 64 profiles can be defined. If the specified TA profile exists, this command changes to the config-ta-<TA-NAME> context for the profile. The no form of this command removes the specified TA profile. NOTE: When creating a new profile, If you exit the config-ta-<TA-NAME> context without importing the TA certificate, the profile is discarded. 158. Syntax: crypto pki ta-profile <TA-NAME>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the crypto pki ta-profile command?
The syntax of the crypto pki ta-profile command is: crypto pki ta-profile <TA-NAME>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the enroll self-signed command do?

The enroll self-signed command generates a key pair and generates a self-signed certificate with it. The subject fields and key type of the current leaf certificate must be defined before running this command. If not, you are prompted to fill in the subject fields, and the key type is set to RSA 2048. Syntax: enroll self-signed.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the enroll self-signed command?
The syntax of the enroll self-signed command is: enroll self-signed.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the enroll terminal command do?

The enroll terminal command generates a key pair and certificate signing request (CSR) for the current leaf certificate. Use the CSR to obtain a signed certificate from a certificate authority (CA), and then import the certificate onto the switch with the command import terminal. The key type, and the certificate common name in the subject fields of the current leaf certificate must be completed before running this command. Imports a CA-signed leaf certificate and then validates the certificate against the specified TA profile. If the imported data includes a private key, the private key must match the leaf certificate being imported. If the imported data does not include a private key, the certificate must match a CSR that was previously 160. Syntax: enroll terminal.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the enroll terminal command?
The syntax of the enroll terminal command is: enroll terminal.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the import terminal self-signed [password <PW>] command do?
The import terminal self-signed [password <PW>] command imports a self-signed leaf certificate including its matching private key. 162. Syntax: import terminal self-signed [password <PW>].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the import terminal self-signed [password <PW>] command?
The syntax of the import terminal self-signed [password <PW>] command is: import terminal self-signed [password <PW>]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the key-type {rsa [key-size <K-SIZE>] | ecdsa [curve-size <C-SIZE>]} command do?

The key-type {rsa [key-size <K-SIZE>] | ecdsa [curve-size <C-SIZE>]} command sets the key type and key size for the current leaf certificate. The key type of the default certificate local- cert cannot be changed. Syntax: key-type {rsa [key-size <K-SIZE>] | ecdsa [curve-size <C-SIZE>]}.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the key-type {rsa [key-size <K-SIZE>] | ecdsa [curve-size <C-SIZE>]} command?
The syntax of the key-type {rsa [key-size <K-SIZE>] | ecdsa [curve-size <C-SIZE>]} command is: key-type {rsa [key-size <K-SIZE>] | ecdsa [curve-size <C-SIZE>]}.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ocsp disable-nonce command do?

The ocsp disable-nonce command configures exclusion of the nonce from OCSP requests. A nonce is a unique identifier that an OCSP client inserts in an OCSP request and expects the OCSP responder to include it in the corresponding OCSP response. The nonce mechanism helps prevent replay attacks in which a malicious player attempts to masquerade as the OCSP responder. Although the nonce is included by default, it can be excluded. Some OCSP responders choose to not support the use of the nonce due to performance considerations. The no form of this command re-enables nonce inclusion in OCSP requests. Syntax: ocsp disable-nonce.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ocsp disable-nonce command?
The syntax of the ocsp disable-nonce command is: ocsp disable-nonce.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ocsp enforcement-level {strict | optional} command do?

The ocsp enforcement-level {strict | optional} command sets either strict or reduced enforcement of the OCSP check of certificates. Strict enforcement is enabled by default. The no form of this command resets enforcement to its default of strict. Syntax: ocsp enforcement-level {strict | optional}.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ocsp enforcement-level {strict | optional} command?
The syntax of the ocsp enforcement-level {strict | optional} command is: ocsp enforcement-level {strict | optional}.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ocsp vrf <VRF-NAME> command do?

The ocsp vrf <VRF-NAME> command configures the OCSP responder URL s that the current TA profile uses to verify the revocation status of an X. 509 digital certificate. These URL s override the OCSP responder URL contained within the peer certificate being verified (as well as URL s defined in any intermediate CAs in the chain of trust). If no OCSP responder URL s are defined for a TA profile (default setting), then the OCSP responder URL in the peer certificate is used for revocation status checking. (The OCSP responder URL is contained in a certificate's Authority Information Access field, which is an X.509 v3 certificate extension.) The no form of this command deletes the specified OCSP responder URL (primary or secondary) from the current TA profile. Syntax: ocsp vrf <VRF-NAME>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ocsp vrf <VRF-NAME> command?
The syntax of the ocsp vrf <VRF-NAME> command is: ocsp vrf <VRF-NAME>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show crypto pki application command do?
The show crypto pki application command is documented in this guide. Syntax: show crypto pki application.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show crypto pki application command?
The syntax of the show crypto pki application command is: show crypto pki application.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show crypto pki certificate command do?

The show crypto pki certificate command shows a list of all configured leaf certificates, or detailed information for a specific leaf certificate. Possible values for Cert Status are: CSR pending, expired, expires soon, installed, malformed, not yet known. Possible values for EST Status are: enroll failed, enroll pending, enroll retrying, enroll success, n/a (certificate is not EST-enrolled), reenroll failed, reenroll pending, reenroll retrying. Syntax: show crypto pki certificate [<CERT-NAME> [plaintext | pem]].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show crypto pki certificate command?
The syntax of the show crypto pki certificate command is: show crypto pki certificate [<CERT-NAME> [plaintext | pem]]

The show crypto pki ta-profile command shows a list of all configured TA profiles, or detailed information for a specific profile. NOTE: This command shows information for both directly-configured TA profiles and TA profiles that were dynamically downloaded from EST servers. Syntax: show crypto pki ta-profile [<TA-NAME>].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show crypto pki ta-profile command?
The syntax of the show crypto pki ta-profile command is: show crypto pki ta-profile [<TA-NAME>]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ta-certificate { [import [terminal]] | import {<REMOTE-URL> | <STORAGE-URL>} } command do?

The ta-certificate { [import [terminal]] | import {<REMOTE-URL> | <STORAGE-URL>} } command imports a CA certificate for use in the current TA profile. The certificate must be in PEM format. The PEM data must be delimited with these lines: -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- -----END CERTIFICATE----- 172. Syntax: ta-certificate { [import [terminal]] | import {<REMOTE-URL> | <STORAGE-URL>} }.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ta-certificate { [import [terminal]] | import {<REMOTE-URL> | <STORAGE-URL>} } command?
The syntax of the ta-certificate { [import [terminal]] | import {<REMOTE-URL> | <STORAGE-URL>} } command is: ta-certificate { [import [terminal]] | import {<REMOTE-URL> | <STORAGE-URL>} }.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the subject [common-name <COMMON-NAME>] [country <COUNTRY>] [locality <LOCALITY>] command do?

The subject [common-name <COMMON-NAME>] [country <COUNTRY>] [locality <LOCALITY>] command sets the subject fields for the current leaf certificate. If the common-name parameter is not specified, then you are prompted to define a value for each field. If a configured value exists for any field, it is presented as the default. The subject fields of the default certificate local-cert cannot be changed. Syntax: subject [common-name <COMMON-NAME>] [country <COUNTRY>] [locality <LOCALITY>].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the subject [common-name <COMMON-NAME>] [country <COUNTRY>] [locality <LOCALITY>] command?
The syntax of the subject [common-name <COMMON-NAME>] [country <COUNTRY>] [locality <LOCALITY>] command is: subject [common-name <COMMON-NAME>] [country <COUNTRY>] [locality <LOCALITY>]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the arbitrary-label <LABEL> command do?

The arbitrary-label <LABEL> command within the EST profile context, configures the generic optional label (also known as arbitrary label) to be concatenated to the EST server URL that is configured with the url command. There is no arbitrary label configured by default. Any existing arbitrary label is replaced by this command. The use of arbitrary labels is optional. RFC 7030 allows the use of arbitrary labels so that one EST server may serve multiple CAs with the same server URL that gets concatenated with different arbitrary labels. The same label is used for every request made under a particular EST profile. Some EST schemes use arbitrary labels in a more sophisticated way, defining different labels for different types of requests under the same EST profile. Syntax: arbitrary-label <LABEL>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the arbitrary-label <LABEL> command?
The syntax of the arbitrary-label <LABEL> command is: arbitrary-label <LABEL>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the arbitrary-label-enrollment <LABEL> command do?

The arbitrary-label-enrollment <LABEL> command within the EST profile context, configures the arbitrary enrollment label to be concatenated to the EST server URL that is configured with the url command. This label is specific to the enrollment operation. There is no arbitrary enrollment label configured by default. Any existing arbitrary enrollment label is replaced by this command. The use of arbitrary enrollment labels is optional. When the enrollment label is not configured, the generic arbitrary label (created with the arbitrary-label command) is used (if configured) for enrollment. RFC 7030 allows the use of arbitrary labels so that one EST server may serve multiple CAs with the same server URL that gets concatenated with different arbitrary labels. The same label is used for every request made under a particular EST profile. Syntax: arbitrary-label-enrollment <LABEL>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the arbitrary-label-enrollment <LABEL> command?
The syntax of the arbitrary-label-enrollment <LABEL> command is: arbitrary-label-enrollment <LABEL>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the arbitrary-label-reenrollment <LABEL> command do?

The arbitrary-label-reenrollment <LABEL> command within the EST profile context, configures the arbitrary re-enrollment label to be concatenated to the EST server URL that is configured with the url command. This label is specific to the re-enrollment operation. There is no arbitrary re-enrollment label configured by default. Any existing arbitrary re-enrollment label is replaced by this command. The use of arbitrary re-enrollment labels is optional. When the re-enrollment label is not configured, the generic arbitrary label (created with the arbitrary- label command) is used (if configured) for re-enrollment. RFC 7030 allows the use of arbitrary labels so that one EST server may serve multiple CAs with the same server URL that gets concatenated with different arbitrary labels. Syntax: arbitrary-label-reenrollment <LABEL>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the arbitrary-label-reenrollment <LABEL> command?
The syntax of the arbitrary-label-reenrollment <LABEL> command is: arbitrary-label-reenrollment <LABEL>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the crypto pki est-profile command do?

The crypto pki est-profile command creates a certificate Enrollment over Secure Transport (EST) profile and changes to the config-est-<EST- NAME> context for the profile. Each EST profile stores information about the EST service, including EST server URL Up to 16 profiles can be created. If the specified EST profile exists, this command changes to the config-est-<EST-NAME> context for the profile. The no form of this command deletes the specified EST profile. It also deletes the TA profiles whose CA certificates were downloaded from the corresponding EST server, and the leaf certificates that were enrolled using this EST profile. NOTE: The deletion of the related TA profiles and enrolled certificates is permanent. Syntax: crypto pki est-profile <EST-NAME>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the crypto pki est-profile command?
The syntax of the crypto pki est-profile command is: crypto pki est-profile <EST-NAME>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the reenrollment-lead-time <LEAD-TIME> command do?

The reenrollment-lead-time <LEAD-TIME> command enrolls a leaf certificate through a remote EST (Enrollment over Secure Transport) server. Per RFC 7030, EST enables clients to request certificate signing services over secure TLS connections. The switch generates a key pair and the corresponding CSR. The CSR is sent to the EST server to request signing, and the signed certificate is be returned to the switch where it is validated. If the whole process succeeds, the certificate can be used as a leaf certificate on the switch. When the leaf certificate approaches its expiry date, it will be renewed automatically through the same EST server. Each enrollment or re-enrollment attempt starts with a /cacerts request sent to the EST server to get the latest chain of CA certificates. Syntax: reenrollment-lead-time <LEAD-TIME>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the reenrollment-lead-time <LEAD-TIME> command?
The syntax of the reenrollment-lead-time <LEAD-TIME> command is: reenrollment-lead-time <LEAD-TIME>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the retry-count <RETRIES> command do?

The retry-count <RETRIES> command within the EST profile context, sets the maximum number of retires to be attempted after the initial certificate enrollment request fails. The no form of this command resets the maximum number of certificate enrollment request retries to its default of 3. Syntax: retry-count <RETRIES>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the retry-count <RETRIES> command?
The syntax of the retry-count <RETRIES> command is: retry-count <RETRIES>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show crypto pki est-profile command do?
The show crypto pki est-profile command shows a list of all configured EST profiles, or detailed information for a specific profile. Syntax: show crypto pki est-profile [<EST-NAME>].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show crypto pki est-profile command?
The syntax of the show crypto pki est-profile command is: show crypto pki est-profile [<EST-NAME>]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the url <URL> command do?

The url <URL> command within the EST profile context, configures the URL of the certificate enrollment EST server. This is not configured by default. Any existing URL is replaced by this command. The no form of this command removes the EST server URL within the selected EST profile. The removal of the URL does not affect the TA profiles and enrolled certificates from the EST server. Syntax: url <URL>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the url <URL> command?
The syntax of the url <URL> command is: url <URL>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the vrf command do?

The vrf command within the EST profile context, selects the VRF through which the EST server can be reached. Any existing VRFselection is replaced by this command. When this command is not used, VRF mgmt is used by default on switch families supporting the mgmt VRF, otherwise the default VRF named default is used. The no form of this command selects the default VRF either mgmt or default. Syntax: vrf <VRF-NAME>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the vrf command?
The syntax of the vrf command is: vrf <VRF-NAME>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what security policy information is documented for MAC sec?

MAC sec: NOTE: MAC sec is exclusive to the 8360 Switch Series, model JL717A. About MAC sec Media Access Control security (MAC sec): • Provides Layer 2 security protecting network communications against a range of attacks including: denial of service, intrusion, man-in-the-middle, and eavesdropping. These attacks exploit Layer 2 vulnerabilities and often cannot be detected. • Provides Layer 2 hop-by-hop encryption on point-to-point Ethernet links. • Enables a bi-directional secure link after an exchange and verification of security keys between two connected devices. • Secures switch-to-switch infrastructure using the MKA (MAC sec Key Agreement) protocol and Static CAK (Connectivity Association Key). • Is intended for wired LANs. More specifically, MAC sec provides: • Connectionless data integrity: Unauthorized changes to data cannot be made without being detected. Each MAC frame carries a separate integrity verification code. • Data origin authenticity: A received MAC frame is guaranteed to have been sent by the authenticated device.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the apply macsec policy command do?

The apply macsec policy command within the selected interface context, applies the specified MAC sec policy to the selected port. When a MAC sec policy is applied to a port, MAC sec is enabled on the port and all data traffic is blocked on the port until a secure channel is successfully established. Syntax: apply macsec policy <MACSEC-POLICY-NAME>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the apply macsec policy command?
The syntax of the apply macsec policy command is: apply macsec policy <MACSEC-POLICY-NAME>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the clear macsec statistics command do?

The clear macsec statistics command clears MAC sec statistics on all MAC sec-enabled interfaces or on a specific interface or interface range. MAC sec statistics are cleared for the entire switch rather than just in the current user session. Syntax: clear macsec statistics [interface <IF-RANGE>].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the clear macsec statistics command?
The syntax of the clear macsec statistics command is: clear macsec statistics [interface <IF-RANGE>]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the macsec policy <MACSEC-POLICY-NAME> command do?

The macsec policy <MACSEC-POLICY-NAME> command creates the specified MAC sec policy and then enters its context (displayed in the CLI as config-macsec- policy). If the MAC sec policy already exists, this command enters the specified MAC sec policy context. A MAC sec policy can be applied to one or more switch ports, enabling MAC sec on the ports. An MKA (MAC sec Key Agreement) policy must be applied to the same ports. The no form of this command deletes the MAC sec policy. NOTE: A MAC sec policy cannot be deleted if it is currently applied to any ports. All application of the policy must be removed before the policy can be deleted. Syntax: macsec policy <MACSEC-POLICY-NAME>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the macsec policy <MACSEC-POLICY-NAME> command?
The syntax of the macsec policy <MACSEC-POLICY-NAME> command is: macsec policy <MACSEC-POLICY-NAME>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the replay-protection [window-size <WINDOW-SIZE>] command do?

The replay-protection [window-size <WINDOW-SIZE>] command within the MAC sec policy context, enables replay protection with the default or specified window size. With replay protection enabled, packets are expected to arrive within the replay protection window number of packets. For example with a window size of 10, any packet arriving out-of-sequence by more than 10 packets will be discarded. A window size of 0 (the default) enforces strict order of packet reception, discarding all packets not received in perfect sequence. The no form of this command disables replay protections and resets the window size to its 0 default. Syntax: replay-protection [window-size <WINDOW-SIZE>].

For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the clear mka statistics command do?

The clear mka statistics command clears MKA statistics on all MAC sec-enabled interfaces or on a specific interface or interface range. MKA statistics are cleared for the entire switch rather than just in the current user session. In the config-mka-policy policy context, configures the MKA key server priority. The highest priority is 0 and indicates that this switch strongly wants to be the MKA key server. The lowest priority is 255 and indicates that switch does not want to be the MKA key server, allowing the switch at the other end of the link to be the key server. Set this priority on the switches at either end of the link to achieve the desired effect. If the key server priority is 0 on both switches then the switch with the lowest system MAC sec address is elected as key server. Syntax: clear mka statistics [interface <IF-RANGE>].

For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the hsc command do?

The hsc command creates a hardware switch controller (HSC) and changes to the config-hsc context for it. The switch uses the HSC to communicate with a remote controller (such as VMware NSX) to create an extended a layer 2 VXLAN network. The HSC runs an open-source OVSDB server. OVSDB is the Open vSwitch Database Management Protocol detailed in RFC 7047. The remote controller connects to the server as an OVSDB client, and is responsible for managing the interaction with the HSC. As a result, the remote controller is known as the HSC manager. The no form of this command removes the HDC controller and deletes all configuration settings. 98 AOS-CX 10.06 VXLAN EVPN Guide. Syntax: hsc.

For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is VSX?

VSX: Aruba Virtual Switching Extension (VSX) is virtualization technology for aggregation/core switches running the AOS-CX operating system. This solution lets the switches present as one virtualized switch in critical areas. Configuration synchronization is one aspect of this VSX solution where the primary switch configuration is synced to the secondary switch. This solution allows for a pseudo single plane of glass configuration and helps keep key configuration pieces in synchronization as operational changes are made. Since the solution is primarily for high availability, it is expected that most of the configuration policy is the same across both peers. VSX virtualizes the control plane of two aggregation switches to function as one device at layer 2 and as independent devices at layer 3. From a datapath perspective, each device does an independent forwarding lookup to decide how to handle traffic. Some of the forwarding databases, such as the MAC and ARP tables, are synchronized between the two devices using a proprietary VSX control plane.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Benefits of VSX?

Benefits of VSX: VSX has similar benefits as Virtual Switching Framework (VSF), however, VSX also offers better high availability required in core and data center environments. VSX binds two AOS-CX switches of the same model type to operate as one device for layer 2. VSX also operates as independent nodes for layer 3. • Control plane: ◦ Dual control plane for better resiliency ◦ Unified management (synchronized configuration and easy troubleshooting) ◦ ◦ Live software upgrade with near zero downtime In-chassis redundancy for the 8400 series switches and device level redundancy for all other platforms, such as for the 832x and 6400 series switches.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is VSX solution topology?

VSX solution topology: • Active gateway support: Active gateways can be configured for active-active routing. VRRP can be used, as an alternative, for active-standby routing. • • ISL links assigned to higher bandwidth: An ISL link has a higher bandwidth compared to VSX links. When planning the topology, consider sizing the ISL link according to the traffic volume required for the east-west traffic of a single-homed VSX during a failover scenario. Increasing resiliency: When creating a LAG with multiple ports on each chassis-based switch, it is a best practice to create the LAG with members from multiple line cards. This technique increases the points of resiliency. • Same VLAN configurations: Both VSX switches have the same VLAN configurations. Make sure that no topology loop is formed because an ISL is added as a member to all the VLANs by default. You can make configuration synchronization automatic between the VSX switches by enabling VSX synchronization.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about VSX LAG?

VSX LAG: VSX LAGs span both aggregation switches. The two switches appear as one device to partner downstream or upstream devices or both when forming a LAG with the VSX pair. The two switches synchronize their databases and states over a user configured link referred to as an Inter-Switch Link (ISL). VSX LAGs are preferable to point-to-point transit VLANs for upstream connectivity when the routed only port is not an option, such with the case of multiple VRFs. This configuration reduces the number of transit VLANs and associated SVIs, simplifying operations and troubleshooting. Enable active forwarding and active gateway to further optimize the traffic path. When you enable active forwarding and active gateway, north- south and south-north traffic bypasses the ISL link. 14.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about VSX solution requirements?

VSX solution requirements: • All VSX switches in an environment must have identical settings for the following: ◦ The VLAN membership for all VSX trunk ports. ◦ The loop protection configuration on a VLAN that is part of a VSX LAG. • Available ports: Make sure that the VSX LAG interface on both the VSX primary and secondary switches has a member port configured and enabled. Make sure that you also have a non-VSX port that is available for the ISL. • Mutually exclusive features:

For NOTE: VSX active-gateway and VRRP can co-exist at global level, the guide documents the following restrictions, notes, or considerations: • Profiles for 832x series switches: All switches must be assigned either in profile L3-agg or L3-core. • Support for Inter-Switch links (ISLs): VSX LAG does not support layer 3 processing, such as a routed port; however, multiple Virtual Switch Interfaces (VSI) can be configured on the switch in association with the VLANs carried over the given VSX LAG. • Support for Layer 3: VSX LAG as a route only port is not supported. To enable Layer 3, create an SVI associated to a given VLAN that is enabled on the VSX LAG. • VLANsupport: The same list of VLANs that are trunked over the VSX LAGs must be configured on the primary and secondary VSX switches in the global configuration. The list of VLANs can be synced to the 16.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about VSX configuration synchronization?

VSX configuration synchronization: simplifies VSX solution management, reduces configuration misconfiguration and drift across VSX peer switches. With configuration synchronization enabled, the primary peer configuration is synced to the secondary peer. This synchronization is controlled in an opt-in manner by enabling VSX synchronization on a section of configuration. If one or more of the following scenarios occur, the secondary switch will receive the configuration update after it fulfills synchronization requirements and is fully enabled: • The secondary switch is not currently present. • The secondary switch is not currently connected to the primary switch through the ISL. • The secondary switch is not currently configured for VSX configuration synchronization at the time VSX configuration synchronization is enabled on the primary switch. You can only enable a specific configuration for syncing through the vsx-sync CLI extension on the primary switch.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Features supporting VSX?

Features supporting VSX: You can enable VSX synchronization at: • The global level: See Enabling VSX synchronization at the global level for a listing of features supporting VSX synchronization at the global level. • The context level: See Enabling VSX synchronization at the context level for a listing of features supporting VSX synchronization at the context level.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about VSX synchronization requirements?

VSX synchronization requirements: • Software image versions must be the same on both switches. • The output from the show vsx status command must show in-sync for Config Sync Status. • Primary and secondary roles configured. • An interswitch link must be configured. • When enabling VSX synchronization under a physical interface, a VLAN interface, or a VSX LAG, create on the secondary switch the physical interface, VLAN interface, or VSX LAG with the same name and routing setting as on the primary switch. For example, if the primary switch has a physical interface of 1/1/1, you must create another physical interface of 1/1/1 on the secondary switch. Also, if the primary VSX switch has routing enabled, the secondary switch must have routing enabled. Once the name and routing information is the same, VSX synchronization synchronizes the additional configuration information from the primary VSX switch to the secondary VSX switch.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Preventing traffic loss?

Preventing traffic loss: Link-up delay When a VSX device is rebooted, it has no entries for MAC, ARP, routes. If downstream VSX LAG ports are activated before the information is relearned, traffic is dropped. To avoid a traffic drop, VSX LAGs on the rebooted device stay down until the restore of LACP, MAC, ARP, and MSTP databases. The learning process has two phases: • Initial synchronization phase: ◦ This phase is the download phase where the rebooted node learns all the LACP+MAC+ARP+STP database entries from its VSX peer through ISLP. ◦ The initial synchronization timer, which is not configurable, is the required time to download the database information from the peer. • Link-up delay phase: ◦ This phase is the duration for: - Installing the downloaded entries to the ASIC. - Establishing router adjacencies with core nodes and learning upstream routes. ◦ The link-up delay timer default value is 180 seconds. ◦ Depending on the network size, ARP/routing tables size, you might be required to set the timer to a higher value (maximum 600 seconds).
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about VSX switch reboot?

VSX switch reboot: linkup-delay-timer linkup-delay-timer exclude lag-list Keepalive scenario The following diagram illustrates a scenario in which both VSX switches are up, but the ISL link is down. The switches cannot exchange information. The keepalive functionality brings down the link between Switch B and Switch C in the following diagram. The traffic is forced to go from Switch C to Switch A and then through the Layer 3 network to access Switch B. The keepalive path is over the Layer 3 network. Traffic traveling from Switch B to Switch A is also forced to go through the Layer 3 network.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Active gateway over VSX?

Active gateway over VSX: Active gateway is a first hop redundancy protocol that eliminates a single point of failure. The active gateway feature is used to increase the availability of the default gateway servicing hosts on the same subnet. An active gateway improves the reliability and performance of the host network by enabling a virtual router to act as the default gateway for that network. If you have enabled active gateway, VRRP is not required. Active gateway is similar to VRRP in that routed traffic from the VSX node is sourced from the switch interface MAC and not the virtual MAC address (VMAC). Each active gateway sends a periodic broadcast hello packet to avoid VMAC aging on the access switches. The switch views the active gateway IP as a self IP address. Active gateway is preferable over VRRP because with VRRP traffic is still pushed over the ISL link, resulting in latency in the network.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about IP multinetting over VSX?

IP multinetting over VSX: IP multinetting is the assignment of more than one IP interface to a single VLAN that is used to enable a router to provide default gateway service to different address ranges associated with a single VLAN. When using IP multinetting in an environment with VSX enabled, you must configure multiple active gateway IP addresses per SVI so that you can reach multiple networks on the same VLAN. Make sure that you configure an IP address for either the primary or secondary VSX switch on the SVI with the same subnet. The maximum number of supported active gateways per switch is 4, 000. Since a maximum of 31 secondary IPv4 addresses can be configured on an SVI, 32 IPv4 active gateways (along with the primary IPv4 address) can be configured per SVI with IP multinetting support. This support is also the same for IPv6 addresses. IMPORTANT: Disable IP ICMP redirect when IP multinetting is enabled.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about VRRP with VSX configuration?

VRRP with VSX configuration: VRRP is similar to active gateway in that it is a first hop redundancy protocol that eliminates a single point of failure. One VSX switch acts as a VRRP master and the other switch acts as the VRRP backup. Both VSX switches route the traffic. The active gateway/VRRP configuration must be consistent across the two VSX switches. Although active gateway and VRRP are no longer globally exclusive in a VSX configuration, active gateway and VRRP are still exclusive on an SVI. A workaround is to configure VRRP on one SVI (SVI A), and configure active-gateway on the other SVI (SVI B). NOTE: Active gateway is preferable to VRRP because VRRP traffic is still pushed over the ISL link, resulting in latency.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about STP over VSX?

STP over VSX: Without a spanning tree, having more than one active path between a pair of nodes causes loops in the network, which can result in duplication of messages, leading to a “broadcast storm” that can bring down the network. STP ensures that only one active path exists between any two nodes in a spanning tree instance. A spanning tree instance comprises a unique set of VLANs, and belongs to a specific spanning tree region. A region can comprise multiple spanning tree instances (each with a different set of VLANs), and allows one active path among regions in a network. NOTE: Spanning-tree guards and filters are not allowed for configuration on the ISL. Supported STP modes The VSX switches support the following spanning tree protocols (STP s) with VSX: • MSTP: Multiple Spanning Tree Protocol. • RPVST: Rapid per-VLANspanning Tree Protocol.

section: VSX at the distribution layer with MSTP enabled

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about VSX at the distribution layer with MSTP enabled?

VSX at the distribution layer with MSTP enabled: In the following figure, the VSX pair is configured as a root switch. All the ports of the VSX LAGs, non-VSX LAGs, and orphan ports are in a forwarding state. Bridge Protocol Data Units (BPDUs), generated by a VSX pair, are the same on all ports, including VSX LAG, non-VSX LAG, and orphan. All switches must be in the same MSTP region consisting of the same configuration name and revision number, as set by the spanning-tree config-name <CONFIG-NAME> and spanning-tree config-revision <REVISION- NUMBER> commands. Switches in the multiple MSTP region consist of different configuration name and revision number. The following example is the extract of MSTP multiple region configuration:

section: Distribution VSX pair connected to the core switch (SVI solution)

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Distribution VSX pair connected to the core switch (SVI solution)?

Distribution VSX pair connected to the core switch (SVI solution): In the following figure, the VSX switch could be either a root switch or a nonroot switch for STP topology. One of the uplinks connected from the distribution layer to the core switch is in a blocking state because the MSTP is enabled in a VSX pair connected to a core switch, but the SVP configured without MSTP is enabled. This configuration might also cause the flooding of the MSTP BPDUs (VLAN unaware) based on the VLAN configuration. VLANs must be configured differently on both ports to avoid flooding back to another VSX.

topic: Distribution VSX pair connected to the core switch (SVI solution)

section: STP interoperability with Loop-Protect in VSX

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about STP interoperability with Loop-Protect in VSX?

STP interoperability with Loop-Protect in VSX: When both loop protect and STP are enabled on the switch: • • • If switch first detects STP, STP blocks the port to stop the loop and loop protect feature will not come into effect. If switch first detects loop protect, loop protect blocks the port to stop the loop and STP will not take any effect as there are no loops. If loop protect has re-enable timer enabled, the port will be unblocked once the timer is expired. In this case, whichever protocol detects the loop first will block the port. Show commands for MSTP IMPORTANT: Before running the show commands, make sure that you have enabled STP synchronization between VSX peer switches. See Enabling VSX synchronization of STP configurations between VSX peer switches.

topic: STP interoperability with Loop-Protect in VSX

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about VSX secondary configuration?

VSX secondary configuration: configure hostname vsx-sec vlan 1, 10, 20 spanning-tree mode rpvst spanning-tree spanning-tree vlan 1, 10, 20 interface mgmt no shutdown ip dhcp interface lag 1 multi-chassis no shutdown no routing vlan trunk native 1 vlan trunk allowed all lacp mode active interface lag 100 no shutdown no routing vlan trunk native 1 vlan trunk allowed all lacp mode active 86.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what restrictions or notes are documented for RPVST with VSX guidelines?

For RPVST with VSX guidelines, the guide documents the following restrictions, notes, or considerations: • Path cost is not allowed to be configured on the ISL port. • Do not configure port-specific spanning tree configurations on the ISL. • Do not have redundant links to the ISL. • Topology changes for VSX LAGs are accounted on the active multichassis LAG role only. • RPVST is supported in both VSX and non-VSX environments. • The common bridge ID continues to be used even after the VSX split brain scenario is identified. • STP configurations on VSX LAG ports must be the same on VSX switches. • To find the maximum supported RPVST instances that can be configured, enter the following command: show capacities rpvst 92.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Loop protect configurations over VSX?

Loop protect configurations over VSX: Loop protect can be enabled on VSX. Loop protect is a switch feature, which is used to identify and prevent layer 2 loops in a network. The loop protect feature blocks the port based on the configured action, these actions may be: • Tx-Disable • Tx-Rx-Disable • Do-Not-Disable See the Layer 2 Bridging Guide for information about loop protect. To set up loop protect with VSX, loop protect must be enabled on the interfaces on the primary and secondary VSX switches.

section: How loop protect works over VSX

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about How loop protect works over VSX?

How loop protect works over VSX: Assume that you have the loop protect feature enabled on lag 1/1/1 on the primary VSX switch and loop protect enabled on lag 1/1/2 on the secondary VSX switch. When a loop occurs, loop protect notifies the secondary VSX switch that a loop is on the network and interface1/1/2 was blocked. When you enter show interface 1/1/2 on the secondary switch, the output from the command indicates that the interface was blocked by VSX when in fact the loop protect feature blocked the interface to stop the loop.

topic: How loop protect works over VSX

section: Best practices for loop protect over VSX

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Best practices for loop protect over VSX?

Best practices for loop protect over VSX: • Enable loop protect on both primary and secondary VSX switches. • Do not enable loop protect on the ISL link for the primary and secondary VSX switches. • • If you enable an action for loop protect, such as do-not-disable, and another action, such as Tx-Rx- Disable, is already in effect, loop protect must be disabled and then re-enabled. If you run the loop-protect action do-not-disable command, on every transmit interval, the loop is detected and the detection is reported through an SNMP trap and an event log message. You can view the events for just the loop protect feature by entering the show events -d hpe-lpd command. • The total number of VLANs across ports is (ports x VLANs) = 4094 ports per VLAN. Loop protect can be configured on a maximum of 4094 VLANs across all interfaces without updating CoPP policies for loop protect. If your network configuration requires you to configure more VLAN, update your CoPP policies values for loop protect to ensure that you allocate more resources.

topic: Best practices for loop protect over VSX

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about EVPN VSX support?

EVPN VSX support: Ethernet VPN (EVPN) is supported with VSX. The two VSX pairs act as independent BGP routing entities to the other VXLAN tunnel endpoints (VTEPs) or spines for control packets. However, in the datapath, both of them act as a single logical VTEP. This is achieved by using different IP addresses for establishing the BGP session and using a common IP as next-hop to represent the VTEP. For more information on EVPN VSX support, see the EVPN VSX support chapter in the VXLAN Guide.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the config-sync disable command do?

The config-sync disable command pauses VSX synchronization. The no form of this command restarts VSX synchronization. Configures a physical port or a LAG as an interswitch link port. Only one port or LAG can be configured to act as an ISL. Once a port is configured as an ISL, it becomes a part of all VLANs in a system. The no form of this command clears the configuration of the interswitch link port from a physical port or a LAG. Syntax: config-sync disable.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the config-sync disable command?
The syntax of the config-sync disable command is: config-sync disable.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the inter-switch-link dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL> command do?

The inter-switch-link dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL> command sets the dead interval for the interswitch link protocol. The dead interval is the amount of time to wait for hellos from a peer before declaring the peer to be dead. The default dead interval time is 20 seconds. The no form of this command resets the interswitch link dead interval to the default of 20 seconds. Syntax: inter-switch-link dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the inter-switch-link dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL> command?
The syntax of the inter-switch-link dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL> command is: inter-switch-link dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the inter-switch-link hello-interval <HELLO-INTERVAL> command do?

The inter-switch-link hello-interval <HELLO-INTERVAL> command configures the interswitch link hello-interval. The hello interval determines the frequency of a hello packet exchange to confirm the control plane of the peer is alive. The default hello-interval is 1 second. The no form of this command sets the interswitch link hello-interval to the default of 1 second. Sets the holdtime for the interswitch link protocol. A port is treated as down only when it stays down for the configured holdtime interval. The default holdtime is 0 seconds. 114. Syntax: inter-switch-link hello-interval <HELLO-INTERVAL>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the inter-switch-link hello-interval <HELLO-INTERVAL> command?
The syntax of the inter-switch-link hello-interval <HELLO-INTERVAL> command is: inter-switch-link hello-interval <HELLO-INTERVAL>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the inter-switch-link peer-detect-interval <PEER-DETECT-INTERVAL> command do?

The inter-switch-link peer-detect-interval <PEER-DETECT-INTERVAL> command sets the amount of time in seconds that the VSX switch waits for the ISL interface to link up after a reboot. If the ISL link does not come up within this time window, the VSX switch declares itself as split from its peer. The default peer detect interval is 300 seconds. The no form of this command sets the interswitch link protocol peer detect interval to the default of 300 seconds. Syntax: inter-switch-link peer-detect-interval <PEER-DETECT-INTERVAL>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the inter-switch-link peer-detect-interval <PEER-DETECT-INTERVAL> command?
The syntax of the inter-switch-link peer-detect-interval <PEER-DETECT-INTERVAL> command is: inter-switch-link peer-detect-interval <PEER-DETECT-INTERVAL>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the interface lag multi-chassis command do?

The interface lag multi-chassis command configures a given LAG as a dynamic multichassis LAG (VSX LAG), which supports a maximum of four member links per switch segment. A VSX LAG across a downstream switch can have at most a total of eight member links. The no form of this command removes a VSX LAG. Syntax: interface lag <LAG-ID> multi-chassis [static].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the interface lag multi-chassis command?
The syntax of the interface lag multi-chassis command is: interface lag <LAG-ID> multi-chassis [static]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the ip icmp redirect command do?

The ip icmp redirect command enables the sending of ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 redirect messages to the source host. Enabled by default. The no form of this command disables ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 redirect messages to the source host. Syntax: ip icmp redirect.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the ip icmp redirect command?
The syntax of the ip icmp redirect command is: ip icmp redirect.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the keepalive dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL> command do?

The keepalive dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL> command sets the dead-interval for keepalive protocol. The dead interval is the amount of time to wait for hellos from a peer before declaring the peer to be dead. The default dead-interval is 3 seconds. The no form of this command sets the interswitch link dead-interval to the default of 3 seconds. Sets the hello-interval for keepalive protocol. The hello interval determines the frequency of a hello packet exchange to confirm the peer is alive. The default hello-interval is 1 second. The no form of this command sets the hello-interval for keepalive protocol to the default of 1 second. 118. Syntax: keepalive dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the keepalive dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL> command?
The syntax of the keepalive dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL> command is: keepalive dead-interval <DEAD-INTERVAL>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the keepalive peer <PEER-IP-ADDR> source <SOURCE-IP-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The keepalive peer <PEER-IP-ADDR> source <SOURCE-IP-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command sets the source and peer IP addresses for keepalive packets in a specified VRF. If a VRF is not specified, it sets to the default VRF. The no form of this command removes the source and peer IP addresses and VRF for the keepalive protocol. VSX continues to work. Syntax: keepalive peer <PEER-IP-ADDR> source <SOURCE-IP-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the keepalive peer <PEER-IP-ADDR> source <SOURCE-IP-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command?
The syntax of the keepalive peer <PEER-IP-ADDR> source <SOURCE-IP-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command is: keepalive peer <PEER-IP-ADDR> source <SOURCE-IP-ADDR> [vrf <VRF-NAME>]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the keepalive udp-port <PORT-NUM> command do?

The keepalive udp-port <PORT-NUM> command sets the UDP port for the keepalive protocol. The no form of this command sets the UDP port for keepalive protocol to the default of 7678. Syntax: keepalive udp-port <PORT-NUM>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the keepalive udp-port <PORT-NUM> command?
The syntax of the keepalive udp-port <PORT-NUM> command is: keepalive udp-port <PORT-NUM>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the lacp fallback command do?

The lacp fallback command sets LACP fallback on a VSX LAG port. When no LACP partner is detected, the VSX LAG port makes members of the VSX LAG function as nonbonded interfaces. To create a VSX LAG, use the interface lag multi- chassis command. The no form of this command sets the VSX LAG to a block state when no LACP partner is detected. Syntax: lacp fallback.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the lacp fallback command?
The syntax of the lacp fallback command is: lacp fallback.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the linkup-delay-timer <DELAY-TIMER> command do?

The linkup-delay-timer <DELAY-TIMER> command configures the VSX link-up delay timer. The VSX delay timer feature lets you configure the delay timer, which delays bringing downstream VSX links up, following a VSX device reboot or an ISL flap. The no form of this command restores the VSX link-up delay timer to a default of 180 seconds. Configures the VSX link-up delay timer exclude list. It excludes the bringing up of specified downstream VSX LAGs, following a device reboot or an ISL flap. The no form of this command unconfigures the VSX link-up delay timer exclude list. Syntax: linkup-delay-timer <DELAY-TIMER>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the linkup-delay-timer <DELAY-TIMER> command?
The syntax of the linkup-delay-timer <DELAY-TIMER> command is: linkup-delay-timer <DELAY-TIMER>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> vsx-sync-exclude command do?

The neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> vsx-sync-exclude command excludes VSX sync for the BGP neighbor. Configures the VSX device role. The no form of this command removes the device role of the switch in VSX and causes the interswitch link to be out-of-sync. Syntax: neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> vsx-sync-exclude.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> vsx-sync-exclude command?
The syntax of the neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> vsx-sync-exclude command is: neighbor <IP-ADDRESS> vsx-sync-exclude.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show active-gateway command do?
The show active-gateway command displays the gateway information configured on SVIs, such as: . Syntax: show active-gateway [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show active-gateway command?
The syntax of the show active-gateway command is: show active-gateway [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show active-gateway <IFNAME> command do?

The show active-gateway <IFNAME> command displays the gateway information per SVI, such as: • Active-Gateway IPv4 and its MAC address • Active-Gateway IPv6 and its MAC address. Syntax: show active-gateway <IFNAME> [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show active-gateway <IFNAME> command?
The syntax of the show active-gateway <IFNAME> command is: show active-gateway <IFNAME> [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show interface <VLAN-NAME> [vsx-peer] command do?
The show interface <VLAN-NAME> [vsx-peer] command displays a virtual IPv4/IPv6 and MAC configured for active-active routing.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show lacp aggregates command do?
The show lacp aggregates command displays a specified LAG or all configured LAGs along with VSX LAGs. Syntax: show lacp aggregates [<LAG-NAME>] [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show lacp aggregates command?
The syntax of the show lacp aggregates command is: show lacp aggregates [<LAG-NAME>] [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show lacp interfaces command do?

The show lacp interfaces command displays an LACP configuration of the physical interfaces, including VSX s. If an interface name is passed as argument, it only displays an LACP configuration of a specified interface. Syntax: show lacp interfaces [<IFNAME>] [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show lacp interfaces command?
The syntax of the show lacp interfaces command is: show lacp interfaces [<IFNAME>] [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show lacp interfaces multi-chassis command do?

The show lacp interfaces multi-chassis command shows all configured VSX remote interface details. The interface that has the ALFNCD status has been synced with the partner and is ready for flow distribution. Syntax: show lacp interfaces multi-chassis [<IFNAME>] [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show lacp interfaces multi-chassis command?
The syntax of the show lacp interfaces multi-chassis command is: show lacp interfaces multi-chassis [<IFNAME>] [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show running-config interface command do?
The show running-config interface command displays all configured interface commands, including VSX commands. Syntax: show running-config interface.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show running-config interface command?
The syntax of the show running-config interface command is: show running-config interface.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show running-config vsx command do?
The show running-config vsx command displays the configured VSX commands. Syntax: show running-config vsx.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show running-config vsx command?
The syntax of the show running-config vsx command is: show running-config vsx.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show running-config vsx-sync command do?

The show running-config vsx-sync command displays the lines of running-configuration that VSX configuration synchronization is enabled on. The command also provides a rolled-up view of configuration expected to be synced. This command can be run from the primary or secondary peer. 134. Syntax: show running-config vsx-sync.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show running-config vsx-sync command?
The syntax of the show running-config vsx-sync command is: show running-config vsx-sync.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show running-config vsx-sync peer-diff command do?

The show running-config vsx-sync peer-diff command displays the difference between the configuration of features enabled for VSX synchronization on the primary and secondary switches. Syntax: show running-config vsx-sync peer-diff.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show running-config vsx-sync peer-diff command?
The syntax of the show running-config vsx-sync peer-diff command is: show running-config vsx-sync peer-diff.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show vsx active-forwarding [interface <INTERFACE-VLAN>] [vsx-peer] command do?

The show vsx active-forwarding [interface <INTERFACE-VLAN>] [vsx-peer] command shows all the VSX active-forwarding configured interface VLANs or the VSX active-forwarding peer information for a particular interface VLAN.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show vsx config-consistency command do?
The show vsx config-consistency command displays the VSX global configuration consistency between two VSX switches. Syntax: show vsx config-consistency [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show vsx config-consistency command?
The syntax of the show vsx config-consistency command is: show vsx config-consistency [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show vsx config-consistency lacp command do?
The show vsx config-consistency lacp command displays VSX LACP configuration consistency between two VSX switches. Syntax: show vsx config-consistency lacp [<LAG-NAME>] [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show vsx config-consistency lacp command?
The syntax of the show vsx config-consistency lacp command is: show vsx config-consistency lacp [<LAG-NAME>] [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show vsx ip data-path command do?

The show vsx ip data-path command displays the datapath of the IPv4 route present on local and VSX peer devices. Syntax: show vsx ip data-path [<IP-ADDR> | <IP-ADDR>/<MASK>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show vsx ip data-path command?
The syntax of the show vsx ip data-path command is: show vsx ip data-path [<IP-ADDR> | <IP-ADDR>/<MASK>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show vsx ip route command do?
The show vsx ip route command displays a specified LAG or all configured LAGs along with VSX LAGs. Syntax: show vsx ip route [<IP-ADDR> | <IP-ADDR>/<MASK> | unique].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show vsx ip route command?
The syntax of the show vsx ip route command is: show vsx ip route [<IP-ADDR> | <IP-ADDR>/<MASK> | unique]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show vsx IPv6 data-path command do?

The show vsx IPv6 data-path command displays the datapath of the IPv6 route on local and peer VSX devices. Syntax: show vsx IPv6 data-path [<IPv6-ADDR> | <IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show vsx IPv6 data-path command?
The syntax of the show vsx IPv6 data-path command is: show vsx IPv6 data-path [<IPv6-ADDR> | <IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>] [vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show vsx IPv6 route command do?
The show vsx IPv6 route command displays a specified LAG or all configured LAGs along with VSX LAGs. Syntax: show vsx IPv6 route [<IPv6-ADDR> | <IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK> | unique].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show vsx IPv6 route command?
The syntax of the show vsx IPv6 route command is: show vsx IPv6 route [<IPv6-ADDR> | <IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK> | unique]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show vsx status command do?
The show vsx status command displays global VSX status or a specified status determined by the selected parameter. Syntax: show vsx status [inter-switch-link | keepalive | linkup-delay] [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show vsx status command?
The syntax of the show vsx status command is: show vsx status [inter-switch-link | keepalive | linkup-delay] [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the show vsx status config-sync command do?

The show vsx status config-sync command displays VSX configuration synchronization status for peers. This command can be run from the primary or secondary peer to view the configuration synchronization state. Syntax: show vsx status config-sync [vsx-peer].
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the show vsx status config-sync command?
The syntax of the show vsx status config-sync command is: show vsx status config-sync [vsx-peer]
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the system-mac <MAC-ADDR> command do?

The system-mac <MAC-ADDR> command enables split recovery mode. Split recovery mode is enabled by default. The no form of this command disables split-recovery mode. Sets the MAC address as the VSX system MAC address to be used by control plane protocols, such as STP and LACP. A pair of VSX switches must have the same VSX system MAC. The no form of this command unconfigures the VSX system MAC address to be used by control plane protocols. Syntax: system-mac <MAC-ADDR>.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the system-mac <MAC-ADDR> command?
The syntax of the system-mac <MAC-ADDR> command is: system-mac <MAC-ADDR>
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the vsx command do?
The vsx command creates the VSX context on the switch. The no form of this command disables the VSX context on the switch and removes all related configuration settings. Syntax: vsx.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the vsx command?
The syntax of the vsx command is: vsx.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the vsx active-forwarding command do?

The vsx active-forwarding command configures VSX active-forwarding on an interface VLAN. The no form of this command unconfigures VSX active-forwarding on a VLAN interface. Syntax: vsx active-forwarding.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the vsx active-forwarding command?
The syntax of the vsx active-forwarding command is: vsx active-forwarding.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]} command do?

The vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]} command enables VSX sync of active gateways or policies associated under an interface. To synchronize the associations, you must configure the same interface vlan on the peer switch. The no form of this command removes VSX synchronization for active gateways or policies associated under an interface, but it does not remove the feature configurations from the secondary peer switch. Syntax: vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]}.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]} command?
The syntax of the vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]} command is: vsx-sync {[active-gateways] [policies]}.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the vsx-sync aaa command do?

The vsx-sync aaa command enables VSX synchronization of all AAA configurations, including user, RADIUS server, and TACACS+ server, on the primary VSX node to the secondary peer switch. To synchronize AAA configurations associated with a particular VRF, you must configure the same VRF on the peer switch. But it does not remove the previously synced configurations from the secondary peer switch. NOTE: The OSPF router ID is not synchronized. This exclusion is needed because the router ID uniquely identifies the router. The two OSPF routers with the same router ID do not form an adjacency between them. Syntax: vsx-sync ospf.

section: Label b: Then, the secondary VSX

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the guide say about Label b: Then, the secondary VSX?

Label b: Then, the secondary VSX: switch brings down the VSX LAG member ports. Active gateway is unreachable Symptom You are unable to ping the active gateway. Action 1. Verify that kernel interface is created for active gateway: 003000000000001@vlan3: <NO-CARRIER, BROADCAST, UP, M-DOWN> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue state LOWERLAYERDOWN group default qlen 1000 link/ether 00: 00: 00: 00: 00: 01 brd ff: ff: ff: ff: ff: ff 2. Verify that the active gateway IP is programmed correctly on kernel interface: 003000000000001@vlan3: <NO-CARRIER, BROADCAST, UP, M-DOWN> mtu 1500 qdisc noqueue state LOWERLAYERDOWN group default qlen 1000 link/ether 00: 00: 00: 00: 00: 01 brd ff: ff: ff: ff: ff: ff inet 10.0.0.3/32 scope global 003000000000001 valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever inet 20.0.0.3/32 scope global 003000000000001 valid_lft forever preferred_lft forever 3. Verify that the active gateway VMAC is correctly configured in the hardware. The command used is platform-specific. Refer to the hardware documentation for your switch. The following example from the 8400 series switch: 8400X: /home/admin# ovs-appctl l3pd/show router-mac -a 196.

topic: Label b: Then, the secondary VSX

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, how do you configure SNMP?

To configure SNMP, follow the documented procedure: 1. Set the system contact, location, and description for the switch with the following commands: 2. If required, change the default SNMP port on which the agent listens for requests with the command. Commands shown in the procedure include: snmp-server vrf mgmt to enable SNMP on the management interface. Use the command snmp-; snmp-server agent-port..
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the event-trap-enable command do?

The event-trap-enable command enables the notification of events to be sent as traps to the SNMP management stations. It is enabled by default. The no form of this command disables the event traps. Syntax: event-trap-enable.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what is the syntax of the event-trap-enable command?
The syntax of the event-trap-enable command is: event-trap-enable.
For 8400 AOS-CX 10.06, what does the rmon alarm index <INDEX> snmp-oid <SNMP-OID> rising-threshold <RISING-THRESHOLD> command do?

The rmon alarm index <INDEX> snmp-oid <SNMP-OID> rising-threshold <RISING-THRESHOLD> command stores configuration entries in an alarm table that defines the sample interval, sample-type, and threshold parameters for an SNMP MIB object. Only the SNMP MIB objects that resolve to an ASN.1 primitive type of INTEGER (INTEGER, Integer32, Counter32, Counter64, Gauge32, or Time Ticks) will be monitored. The no form of this command removes all RMON alarms and allows you to specify an index to remove a particular RMON alarm. Enables and disables the RMON alarm and its index. RMON alarm is enabled by default. Syntax: rmon alarm index <INDEX> snmp-oid <SNMP-OID> rising-threshold <RISING-THRESHOLD>.
